coredata.dc:title: Water-based graphene quantum dots dispersion as a high-performance long-term stable nanofluid for two-phased closed thermosyphons
coredata.prism:publicationName: International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer
coredata.dc:creator: Soleymaniha, Mohammadreza
Amiri, Ahmad
Shanbedi, Mehdi
Chew, Bee Teng
Wongwises, Somchai
coredata.dc:description: Water-based graphene quantum dots (GQD) suspension has great potential for different heat transfer applications as a novel coolant due to their unique colloidal stability, high thermal conductivity and low penalty for rheological properties once loading GQD. To this end, graphene quantum dots were firstly prepared through a new and cost-effective exfoliation procedure. Based on the morphological characterization, the average thickness and diameter of the synthesized amine treated-GQD (AGQD) were determined as mostly less than 1 nm and in the range of 5–20 nm, respectively. Case studies show that water-based AGQD nanofluid at very low weight fractions shows a considerably higher thermal conductivity than that of base fluid. In a detailed rheological investigation of the water-based AGQD nanofluid, no noteworthy increase was observed in comparison with the base fluid, which is considered as a major benefit for this novel generation of coolants. The water-based AGQD nanofluids were also found to be especially more effective in the thermosyphon in terms of overall thermal properties such as net heat transfer, and thermal efficiency, and rheological property such as effective viscosity, as well as, total pressure drop in comparison to the distilled water. Since the water-based AGQD nanofluids show no sedimentation, high thermal conductivity and fairly no effect on rheological properties, it would provide an economical approach for enhancing the performance of industrial heat pipes and thermosyphons.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Graphene quantum dots
Nanofluids
Heat transfer
Thermosyphon
Thermal performance

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: International Communications in Heat and Mass Transfer

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: AFM ichnography and cross-section contour of A-GQD. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [31].
Viscosity of the water-based A-GQD nanofluids as a function of temperature and concentration for a shear rate of 300 s−1. Reprinted with permission from Ref. [31].
Thermal efficiency of TPCT for water-based A-GQD nanofluids at different weight concentrations as well as water.
The average temperature difference (ΔT) between evaporator and condenser sections as a function of input power and concentration of A-GQD.
Effect of input power and concentration of A-GQD on the average temperature of the evaporator section in a TPCT.
Thermal resistance of the TPCT in the presence of water-based A-GQD nanofluids and water.
Overall heat transfer coefficient of the TPCT in the presence of water-based A-GQD nanofluids and water.
The vacuum pressure drop of the TPCT in the presence of water-based A-GQD nanofluids and water.
Thermal conductivity of water-based A-GQD nanofluid at different weight concentrations and temperatures.
The average increase in the viscosity of water-based A-GQD nanofluids compared with deionized water.

row.entry: Rate % of increase in viscosity [100 ∗ ((μnf − μbf) / μbf)]

head.ce:title: Water-based graphene quantum dots dispersion as a high-performance long-term stable nanofluid for two-phased closed thermosyphons

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, Texas A&M University, College Station, TX, USA

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Mechanical Engineering, University of Malaya, Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia

ce:affiliation.ce:label: d
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Fluid Mechanics, Thermal Engineering and Multiphase Flow Research Lab (FUTURE), Department of Mechanical Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, King Mongkut's University of Technology Thonburi, Bangkmod, Bangkok, Thailand

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Fluid Mechanics, Thermal Engineering and Multiphase Flow Research Lab (FUTURE)
King Mongkut's University of Technology Thonburi, Bangkmod

ce:affiliation.ce:label: e
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: The Academy of Science, The Royal Institute of Thailand, Sanam Suea Pa, Dusit, Bangkok 10300, Thailand

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Water-based graphene quantum dots (GQD) suspension has great potential for different heat transfer applications as a novel coolant due to their unique colloidal stability, high thermal conductivity and low penalty for rheological properties once loading GQD. To this end, graphene quantum dots were firstly prepared through a new and cost-effective exfoliation procedure. Based on the morphological characterization, the average thickness and diameter of the synthesized amine treated-GQD (AGQD) were determined as mostly less than 1 nm and in the range of 5–20 nm, respectively. Case studies show that water-based AGQD nanofluid at very low weight fractions shows a considerably higher thermal conductivity than that of base fluid. In a detailed rheological investigation of the water-based AGQD nanofluid, no noteworthy increase was observed in comparison with the base fluid, which is considered as a major benefit for this novel generation of coolants. The water-based AGQD nanofluids were also found to be especially more effective in the thermosyphon in terms of overall thermal properties such as net heat transfer, and thermal efficiency, and rheological property such as effective viscosity, as well as, total pressure drop in comparison to the distilled water. Since the water-based AGQD nanofluids show no sedimentation, high thermal conductivity and fairly no effect on rheological properties, it would provide an economical approach for enhancing the performance of industrial heat pipes and thermosyphons.

ce:section.ce:para: One of the important equipment in the electronics industry, thermal and electrical applications, is various kinds of heat pipes. Furthermore, heat pipes provide the advantage of energy-efficient vehicles with the minimal requirements for a long-term maintenance [1]. The properties of the apparatus such as the dimension, temperature of base fluid, heat flux, and working fluid are the key factors considered in designing heat pipes [2]. In this context, heat pipes can be divided into four main different groups: loop heat pipe, heat pipe with wick, thermosyphon heat pipe, and thermosyphon loop. Based on the heat transfer rate, the thermal yield, the installation reason, and the objective device to be cooled, different kinds of heat pipes are selected for a particular application [3].

Earlier reports demonstrated that the thermal performance of the thermosyphon heat pipe depends on different factors, including base fluids, right filling ratio to the evaporator volume, design, bend angle and bent position [4,5]. As the key essential issues to achieve an improvement in the performance of thermosyphons, different filling ratios, geometry, working fluids, diameters, bend angles, rotation and vibration systems have been investigated [6–10]. In spite of the fact that all innovations mentioned above resulted in a positive impact on the thermal performance of thermosyphon heat pipe, the most cost-effective factor may be the use of high thermally conductive working fluid, particularly for those systems that are already manufactured [11,12].

As mentioned previously, the thermal performance of the thermosyphon heat pipes is affected by working fluids and this subject has been studied comprehensively [13–17]. Moreover, earlier reports [18–20] confirmed that the thermal performance of the heat pipes is increased by using nanofluids. In a study by Choi [21], the addition of copper nanoparticles into the base fluids resulted in an enhancement in thermal conductivity. In another study, Zeinali Heris et al. [22] studied the convective heat transfer coefficient of water-based CuO nanofluids and made a comparison with the results of water-based Al2O3 nanofluid. A higher convective heat transfer coefficient has been obtained for water-based Al2O3 nanofluids as compared to the water-based CuO nanofluids. In another approach, Shanbedi et al. [5] investigated the performance of a two-phase closed thermosyphon (TPCT) in the presence of multiwalled carbon nanotubes (MWCNT). They were observed ca. 11% increase in the thermal efficiency of the TPCT at 90 kW. In a study by Amiri et al. [1], a water-based GNP nanofluid was prepared in the presence of graphene nanoplatelets (GNP) and a rapid microwave-assisted procedure was used for covalent functionalization of GNPs with carboxylic groups to gain higher stability. According to the results, a significant enhancement in total performance of TPCT was achieved.

Despite all the promising properties of carbon nanostructures such as extremely high thermal conductivity and superior stability in the presence of covalently and non-covalently functionalization, they have one or two micro size dimensions. These micro dimensions may result in some failures such as erosion, chocking loops, etc. and they cannot be used in miniature thermosyphons. Alternatively, most of the metal and metal oxide nanoparticles with nanometer dimensions exhibit the difficulty of long-term stability. This problem is intensified by the lack of potential for performing covalent functionalization. So, introducing a carbon-based nanoparticle with the ability of covalent functionalization to solve the problem of colloidal stability and being in nanometer scale (in diameter and length) can be a solution herein. Fascinating properties of GQDs including fully nano-sized dimensions, high specific surface area [23], great stability in aqueous media [24] and promising thermophysical properties can be an excellent candidate to solve all the mentioned difficulties with the previous nanofluids. To investigate the effect of GQDs on the TPCT performance, in the present study, we synthesized the GQDs with a simple method based on covalent reactions. Due to the small size particles and the presence of covalently-functional groups, the colloidal stability of water-based GQDs was magnificent. The water-based GQDs samples were then characterized and the thermal performance in a thermosyphon was evaluated. The presented results showed that thermo-physical property of water-based GQDs nanofluids were outstanding as compared to the other nanofluids. Overall heat transfer, thermal efficiency, thermal resistance and pressure drop showed significant increases in the presence of water-based GQDs nanofluids even at a very low concentration.

Herein, a modified Hummers method was employed to prepare the graphite oxide sheets from natural graphite powder [25]. In order to prepare GQDs, the method reported by Zhang et al. [25] with slight modification was used. Typically, 2.0 g of the graphite oxide were oxidized in the concentrated HNO3 (60 mL, 38 wt%) and H2SO4 (180 mL, 98 wt%) for 48 h under mild ultrasonication. After cooling, the mixture was then diluted with 1200 mL of deionized water and subsequently was centrifuged at 4000 rpm to separate un-exfoliated material. The supernatant was then diluted with the deionized water. The colloidal suspension was then dialyzed using a dialysis bag (Mw cut off: 3500 Da) overnight.

To remove the acidic groups decorated on the edge of GQDs, 100 mL of colloidal solution was mixed with deionized water (100 mL) and ammonia solution (120 mL) in a vessel. The resulting black suspension was sonicated in a 300 W probe-sonicator for 30 min. The mixture was then stirred for 4 h at 100 °C. After cooling to room temperature, the resulting black suspension centrifuged at 25000 rpm and the supernatant was collected. Once performing the reaction step, a majority of oxidized GQDs changed to the amine-treated GQDs (A-GQDs). Finally, a rotary evaporator was used to concentrate the A-GQDs solution at 50 °C, following by drying in a vacuum oven to obtain the dried A-GQDs powder.

The schematic of the experimental apparatus applied in the present study is depicted in Fig. 1 . The employed TPCT included a copper tube with the inner diameter of 20 mm and a length of 1000 mm, which provide three sections: the evaporator, the condenser, and the adiabatic section. The lengths of evaporator, condenser and adiabatic sections are 350, 400 and 250 mm respectively. In the evaporator section, an electric heater was applied to generate power. The condenser section comprised a shell as a heat exchanger with a 40 mm diameter and 400 mm long surrounding the main pipe with an inner diameter of 20 mm. Four K-type thermocouples were installed on the evaporator part and another four in the condenser section with the same gaps. Also, to have the temperatures of input and output water in the shell, two K-type thermocouples were utilized.

All the sections were isolated with a fireproof blanket having a thickness of 20 mm. Other parts of the experimental setup include a power supply, a water storage tank, and a vacuum pump in order to remove the non-condensate gases from the TPCT. In order to control the temperature of the storage tank, a heating and cooling section was employed. The flow rate of the input water in condenser was kept at 250 mL/min with ±1.0% margin of errors. In addition, the evaluation of various thermal parameters was achieved with the aid of the ammeter with 0.001 A margin of error, thermocouples with 0.1 °C margin of error and voltmeter with 0.1 V margin of error.

The characterization of the A-GQD dispersions was investigated using Atomic force microscopy (AFM, ScanAsyst mode, frequency 1 Hz, Bruker). The thermal conductivity has been determined by a KD2 Pro thermal analyzer (Decagon Devices, USA). The thermal analyzer works according to transient of hot wire technique, and it has the accuracy of about 4%. The rheological behavior of water-based A-GQD nanofluids was determined using a shear-rate controlled Anton Paar rotational rheometer (model Physica MCR301, Anton Paar GmbH) based on the double gap DG 26.7 measuring system.

The thermal conductivity and viscosity of the water-based A-GQD nanofluids were experimentally studied and compared with the Di-water. To investigate the heat transfer rate of the thermosyphon in the presence of different working fluids, the thermal resistance, the thermal efficiency, and overall heat transfer coefficient were measured. To this end, Eq. (1) was applied to measure the input power at the evaporator section.
(1)
Q in
= VI
where V and I are the voltage and current, respectively. The output power at the condenser section (Qout) can be calculated using the Eq. (2). That is,
(2)
Q out
=
m ̇
C p
T out
−
T in
where
m ̇
, Tin and Tout are the mass flow rate, the temperature of the inlet and outlet water to the condenser, respectively. Also, the thermal efficiency is the ratio of output power to input power and is calculated with the aid of the Eq. (3) [26],
(3)
η 1
=
Q out
Q in

The overall thermal resistance of the thermosyphon (Rth) is calculated based on the input power, the average temperature difference between the condenser and the evaporator sections. That is [5],
(4)
R th
=
T ¯
e
−
T ¯
c
Q in
where
T ¯
e
and
T ¯
c
are the average temperature of the evaporator and condenser sections, respectively. The overall heat transfer coefficient (U) can be measured using Eq. (5) [5],
(5)
U =
Q in
πDL
T ¯
e
−
T ¯
c

In Eq. (5), D and L are the diameter and the length of the evaporator section, respectively. According to the second law of thermodynamics, the ratio of energy at the condenser to evaporator sections was introduced as the thermal efficiency of thermosyphon. That is [27],
(6)
η 2
=
e out
e in
=
η 1
1 −
1
T ¯
e
∆ T − 1
T ¯
e
T out
− 1
where ΔT is the average temperature difference between the evaporator and condenser parts, which is calculated by ΔT = Tout − Tin. It is noteworthy to mention here that the method described by Kline and McClintock was employed to evaluate the uncertainties of the measured parameters [28]. The maximum uncertainty of less than 4.5% was evaluated for all the parameters described above.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) was utilized to check the morphology of A-GQD including the thickness and size (Fig. 2 ). In order to control the size of the A-GQDs, a dialysis process was employed. From Fig. 2, the A-GQDs' diameters were typically in the range of 5–20 nm. Moreover, based on the topographic results, the average height of less than 1 nm has been obtained that was similar to the thickness of mono layered GQDs or chemically treated graphene nanoribbons with one layer [29,30]. The cross-section contour of A-GQD shows that a majority of the A-GQDs have only one layer. Further, the cross-section contour of A-GQD also confirmed the preparation of uniform A-GQDs.

The thermal conductivity of water-based A-GQD nanofluids for weight concentrations of 0.001% and 0.002% at various temperatures has been listed in Table 1 . It is clear that water-based A-GQD nanofluids show a higher thermal conductivity than that of water. Furthermore, as the concentration of A-GQD loaded in the basefluid increases, the rate of enhancement in the thermal conductivity increases. The main mechanism of thermal conductivity enhancement in nanofluids may be attributed to the Brownian motion of the suspended particles which is one of the most dominating functions of temperature [32,33]. Several researchers found that based on the micro-convection model, the Brownian motion of the nanoparticles in the base fluid induced local convection [34,35]. Shima et al. [35] showed that Reynolds number in Brownian motion has the inverse proportional with the square root of the particle diameter. Consequently, as the particle size decreases the thermal conductivity will increase significantly. So, owning the nano-size dimensions can intensify the Brownian motion of A-GQDs, leading higher micro-convection and thermal conductivity.

Based on Table 1, it was also confirmed that the thermal conductivity of water-based A-GQD nanofluids is a function of temperature. An enhancement in the temperature resulted in an increase in the thermal conductivity for both the samples, having a higher rate of enhancement at 0.002 wt%, which was explained above.

The viscosity of a working fluid plays a key role in its potential for heat transfer applications. So, the measured viscosity of the water-based A-GQD nanofluids against temperature for a shear rate of 300 s−1 at different concentrations and temperatures has been illustrated in Fig. 3 . First of all, water-based A-GQD nanofluids have insignificantly higher viscosity than that of water at both concentrations and all the temperatures. Expectedly, by increasing temperature, the viscosity of water-based A-GQD nanofluids decreased at various concentrations.

The rate of enhancement in viscosity of the nanofluids in comparison with the deionized water is listed in Table 2 . It can be seen that the maximum increase in viscosity was less than 4%, representing insignificant change in the rheological behavior of base fluid. It is logical because of two main reasons: i). Employing low concentration of additive and ii). Owning nano-sized dimensions, which can minimize the friction between base fluid layers.

Various input powers of 30.1, 59.7, 90.5, 119.9, and 149.8 W were applied to evaluate the thermal efficiency of the TPCT filled with water-based A-GQD nanofluids as well as deionized water. Fig. 4 shows the results of measured efficiency data. It was observed that the thermal efficiency of the TPCT improved significantly once utilizing water-based A-GQD nanofluids. In fact, when a nanofluid such as water-based A-GQD nanofluid uses, the bubble size produced at the evaporator interface decreases as compared to the pure base fluid, resulting higher thermal efficiency of TPCT.

Furthermore, as the weight concentration of A-GQDs increases, the enhancement rate of thermal efficiency increases. It can be attributed to the higher thermal conductivity of nanofluids with higher weight concentration of A-GQD that that of one with lower weight concentration. In a similar way, there is an enhancement in the thermal efficiency of thermosyphon as the input power increases, though the increasing trends depend on the concentrations. Generally, a larger gradient of thermal efficiency (with respect to input power) was obtained for all water-based nanofluids and deionized water at lower input powers in comparison to the higher input power [5]. According to [5], the geyser-effect phenomenon (impulsive boiling at low pressures) is the main reason for the reduction of the thermal efficiency of TPCT at the higher input power of 90 W in the presence of water-based carbon nanostructures nanofluid as a working fluid. Interestingly, for the water-based A-GQD nanofluids, this phenomenon was not as strong as the cases with the water-based graphene nanofluids and water-based carbon nanotubes nanofluids.

Also, the prepared water-based A-GQD nanofluids exhibit higher thermophysical properties such as thermal conductivity in comparison to base fluid. Therefore, the related enhancement in micro-convection can be explained by the more enhancement in the thermal conductivity of water-based A-GQD nanofluids at 0.002 wt% in comparison with 0.001 wt% [34,35]. Moreover, it is clear that many complementary parameters including nucleation site density, bubble size, and the average rise velocity of single bubbles affect the thermal efficiency. Another probable mechanism can be explained based on the bubble diameter and nucleation site density [1]. In this case, the generated vapor bubbles readily burst during their production as a result of contact with nanoparticles surfaces. Also, the preparation of bubble layers in the liquid-solid interface can be another cause of the decrease of heat transfer rate which could be prevented by employing nanoparticles. According to the results, maximum thermal efficiency of 93.1% was achieved for the 0.002 wt% of water-based A-GQD nanofluid at power of 150 W, representing almost 11.5% enhancement as compared to the Di-water at the same operational conditions.

The measured average temperature difference (ΔT) between condenser temperature and evaporator temperature is shown in Fig. 5 for various input powers and concentrations. A decrease in the trend of ΔT with concentration of A-GQD and an increase in the trend of ΔT with the input power are observed in this figure. As mentioned above, the higher bubble size generated in the evaporator section can induce higher thermal resistance on all liquid-solid surfaces of evaporator, leading a lower heat transfer rate [36]. Consequently, there will be a lower heat load on the evaporator in the absence of bubbles, while the production of bubble layer on the evaporator surface results in a higher heat load on the evaporator, leading to an enhancement in the surface temperature of evaporator and as a result larger ΔT.

The changes in the temperature of evaporator section with input power and concentration of A-GQD in di-water are depicted in Fig. 6 . A significant drop in the surface temperature of evaporator section is observed with the increased concentration of A-GQD. Note that the evaporator temperature showed an upward trend with the input power. The decrease in the evaporator temperature resulted from the enhancement of A-GQD concentration may be ascribed to the increase in the rate of the bubble burst in the presence of more A-GQD. Also, A-GQD nanoparticles prevent the production of air layer at the liquid-solid interfaces in the evaporator. So, this trend is in complete agreement with the thermal efficiency results illustrated in Fig. 5. This means that the production of smaller bubbles leads to higher heat transfer rate and eventually higher thermal efficiency than the base fluids.

A comparison of the overall thermal resistance of water-based A-GQD nanofluids at different concentrations with that of deionized water is shown in Fig. 7 for various input powers. Obviously, the overall thermal resistance is reduced with the enhancement of input power particularly for the input power range of less than 90 W. Furthermore, the overall thermal resistance decreases significantly with increasing the concentration of A-GQD in water from 0.001 to 0.002 wt%. It is noteworthy to mention that basefluids preferably have a high thermal conductivity to minimize the temperature gradient, which was also repeated in water-based A-GQD nanofluids at 0.002 wt% compared to sample at 0.001 wt%. The enhancement of fluid thermal conductivity can be the main reason of increasing of efficiency with the rising of nanoparticles' concentration in nanofluids [37].

As presented in Fig. 7, water-based A-GQD nanofluid with concentration of 0.002 wt% (the highest concentration studied) has the lowest thermal resistance of the thermosyphon. This observation is in agreement with the evaporator temperature variations.

The overall heat transfer coefficient of thermosyphon for water-based A-GQD nanofluids at the weight concentrations of 0.002 and 0.001% and different input powers were presented in Fig. 8 . It was observed that with the enhancement of the input power and A-GQD concentration in base fluid, the overall heat transfer coefficient was increased. From Fig. 8, the largest enhancement in overall heat transfer coefficient was obtained in the presence of water-based A-GQD nanofluids at 0.002 wt%. After loading 0.002 wt% and 0.001 wt% of A-GQD in Di-water, the overall heat transfer coefficient of thermosyphon increased almost 38.34% and 24.20% in average, respectively. Loading A-GQD in base fluid at weight concentrations of 0.002% and 0.001% resulted in the highest overall heat transfer coefficient of 508.85 and 455.25 Wm−2 °C−1, respectively.

The results of the measured vacuum pressure drop of the TPCT are experimentally obtained and plotted in Fig. 9 for deionized water and water-based A-GQD nanofluids at various input powers and concentrations. Despite the decrease in vacuum pressure was fairly small for different concentrations, as the concentration of A-GQD in base fluid and the input power increased, the vacuum pressure drop is increased. Taking into consideration these observations, it is verified that the loading of A-GQD in Di-water has a small effect on the viscosity of samples and subsequently on the pressure drop.

Furthermore, the variation of pressure drop with the input power exhibited fairly the same slope with the increase of A-GQD concentration. The maximum pressure drop variation was obtained in the presence of the water-based A-GQD nanofluid using the input power of ~ 30 W at the concentration of 0.002 wt%. After loading 0.001 and 0.002 wt% of A-GQD in Di-water, the pressure drop showed the average increases of 1.006% and 1.841%, respectively. It should be noted that the rates of increase in the pressure drop were insignificant for all the operational conditions after loading A-GQD, which can be resulted from applying 2D nano-sized particles. Also, similar trends were observed for viscosity curves which may be attributed to the direct connection between viscosity and pressure drop.

In the present study, water-based A-GQD nanofluids were prepared with the aid of a relatively rapid approach. The evaluation of the thermal performances of the water-based A-GQD nanofluids and comparison with Di-water have been done for a range of input powers and two concentrations of 0.001 and 0.002 wt%. Acid treatment and following amidation reaction resulted in the formation of abundant carboxyl and amine groups on GQDs surface. The attachment of these functional groups with GQDs afforded outstanding colloidal stability. Higher thermo-physical properties were observed in the synthesized water-based A-GQD nanofluids in comparison with the distilled water. Based on the experimental data, it was proposed that the thermal conductivity of water-based A-GQD nanofluids was considerably increased in comparison with that of base fluid. The measurement of thermal efficiency, overall heat transfer, thermal resistance, and pressure drop of covalent nanofluids exhibited excellent enhancement at very low weight concentrations of A-GQD. The obtained results propose that water-based A-GQD nanofluids show a promising thermal performance and can be considered as the next generation of TPCTs' coolant. Moreover, it was concluded that the addition of A-GQD to the deionized water resulted in a negligible enhancement in the rheological property and pressure drop in TPCT, which has the benefit for potential use instead of low-thermally conductive base fluids.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge RU Geran - Fakulti Program Grant RF003A-2018, Faculty of Engineering, University of Malaya, Malaysia for support to conduct this research work. Authors would like to express their sincere gratitude to the research team of VCN Materials Co., Ltd.
